name: Fruit Liqueur
recipeInstructions:
1. For beautiful and much appreciated gifts, save pretty wine (or similar
bottles) to put this in! Use cheap vodka and brandy; no one will know the
difference with all of the added fruit and sugar. Any kind of soft fruit
will make a delicious product. Blackberries, raspberries, peaches or
apricots are especially good; if using larger fruit like peaches or
apricots, chop before proceeding.
2. Put fruit and sugar in a non-reactive container and mash. Let stand about
1 hour to release juices.
3. Mix in vodka and brandy and pour into a glass container. Cover and let
'mellow' the flavor in a cool dark place for at least a month to allow the
alcohol level to rise sufficiently, stopping fermentation. Do not seal too
tightly; the mixture may bubble until fermentation stops.
4. To check for continued fermentation before sealing, gently mix and then
observe after several hours—there should be no new small bubbles clinging to
the inside of the glass on top of the liquid.
5. Strain out fruit and bottle. For a clearer product, strain through several
layers of dampened cheesecloth or a jelly bag. For crystal clarity,
filter through an unbleached or well-rinsed coffee filter; this is prettier
in a clear bottle but slower than using a jelly bag.
6. For a classy gift presentation, cork the bottles and seal with wax. Put a
length of decorative ribbon up the neck of the bottle, over the cork, and
down the other side of the neck. Fasten the ends lightly below the cork with
masking tape to control the ribbon while dipping in wax.
7. Melt old colored candle stubs or parafin plus a few crayons in a double
boiler (metal can with wax on a rack above water). Dip the top of the bottle,
allowing each coat to cool completely before applying the next.
8. Never leave melting wax unattended on the stove or heat it until it
smokes; overheated wax can explode, causing severe burns.
9. When bottles are cool, remove masking tape and trim the ribbon end so it
extends about an inch below the wax seal. A decorative label from Dover Books
or Williams Sonoma adds a nice touch.
